name: Dimensional Weight Divisors by Carrier
description: Volumetric (DIM) divisors used to convert shipment volume to billable weight. NOT in the knowledge base; the corpus only defines the concept (volume / minimum density requirement). Candidate values below are common industry figures and MUST be confirmed against current carrier rate cards before use.
review_frequency: quarterly (carriers revise divisors, often at annual GRI)
source: TO CONFIRM against a dated capture: FedEx 'What is dimensional weight?' page (fedex.com/en-us/shipping/packaging/what-is-dimensional-weight.html); UPS 'Shipping Dimensions and Weight' page (ups.com/us/en/support/shipping-support/shipping-dimensions-weight); DHL Express volumetric weight calculator page (dhlexpress.ee); USPS Postal Explorer QSG 150; IATA TACT for air.
sourcing_notes: Confirm each divisor and its unit against the carrier's current service guide. Divisors change (e.g. US domestic parcel moved 194 -> 166 -> 139 in^3/lb historically). Record effective_date per carrier and set status to verified once confirmed. Note whether the divisor applies to all packages or only above a size threshold. Sourcing pack findings (checked 2026-07-02, pending formal verification): FedEx official page states 139 in^3/lb for US/Puerto Rico/international packages; UPS official page states 139 for Daily Rates and 166 for Retail Rates (so the 'international parcel 166' row label here is imprecise — 166 is a UPS Retail/US-retail figure, and DHL Express publishes 139 in^3/lb internationally); DHL Express publishes 5000 cm^3/kg metric; USPS currently uses 166 in^3/lb (Postal Explorer QSG 150) with a Federal Register final rule moving it to 139 effective 2026-07-12; the 6000 cm^3/kg air figure is the IATA TACT convention.
